title: Writing - Part 1: Technology and Social Interaction
description: Your class has attended a lecture on the impact of social media on communication in society.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the impacts in your notes. You should explain which impact is more significant, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: It’s wonderful to be able to talk to anyone, anywhere, instantly.
People spend more time on their phones than talking to the person next to them.
We are giving away too much personal data without realising it.

structure.introduction: Briefly state how social media has transformed human interaction.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the benefits of global connectivity.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the potential decline in the quality of face-to-face relationships.
structure.conclusion: Decide which impact has had a more profound effect on society.

content.modelAnswer:
The advent of social media has undoubtedly revolutionised the way in which humans interact. While these platforms have brought people closer in some ways, they have also fundamentally altered the nature of our social bonds. This essay will discuss the dual impacts of global connectivity and the changing quality of interpersonal relationships.

One of the most undeniable benefits of digital platforms is global connectivity. Never before in human history has it been so effortless to maintain relationships across vast distances. For expatriates or international students, social media acts as a vital lifeline to their home cultures, mitigating feelings of isolation. Furthermore, these tools allow for the rapid exchange of ideas, fostering international cooperation and a sense of a global community that transcends traditional borders.

However, this breadth of connection often comes at the expense of depth. There is growing concern that the quality of our face-to-face relationships is diminishing as a result of digital distraction. It is common to see groups of friends in social settings all focused on their respective devices rather than engaging with one another. This 'phubbing' phenomenon can lead to a superficial level of interaction, where the quantity of digital 'likes' is prioritised over meaningful, nuanced conversation.

In conclusion, while the ability to connect globally is a remarkable technological achievement, I believe the impact on the quality of our personal relationships is more significant. The subtle erosion of our ability to be fully present with one another represents a more profound shift in the fabric of society. Technology should ideally supplement our social lives, not replace the richness of physical presence.
